title: What is Ontology and Its Role in Agentic Experience Design?
author: Madonnalisa Chan

sections.heading: Introduction
sections.text: When you stream music or movies and get suggested playlists or genres based on your habits, you’re benefitting from a type of knowledge organization system called ontology. Maybe you’ve come across this term recently. It’s a hot topic becauseagentic AIrelies on a map ofmetadata relationships– the data about the data – in order to make decisions. So, what is ontology, how does it work, and why does it matter? Let’s dig into the basics of this discipline and its role in agentic experience design. What is ontology?Ontologies structure concepts and informationAnatomy of an ontologyInvisible but crucial to innovation

sections.heading: What is ontology?
sections.text: In the world of AI, ontology is a system for organizing information so computers can understand and use that data to make decisions on the output. To state this more formally, an ontology structures concepts and information through the description of classes, properties, attributes, relationships, and logical axioms. Ontologies form data models for knowledge graphs, ensuring consistency and understanding. If that feels academic, it’s because it is. Ontology actually evolved from a philosophical discipline — basically, categorizing things in the universe to understand how they relate to one another. Today, it’s a practical tool for structuring knowledge. It’s now related to information andlibrary science, or the study of how to manage, organize, access, preserve, and share information. (Ask a librarian about ontology and watch them light up.) Without ontologies, your experience using any number of digital platforms would be chaotic. Here are two familiar examples of well-designed ontologies that shape your user experience (UX) and enjoyment: Spotify has ontologies for music genres, with lots of metadata about albums, artists, and how they’re organized in various playlists. These ontologies help power so many rich playlists and stations where they can dynamically generate these experiences purely based on what music you’ve already engaged in.Netflix optimizes its recommendation engines with the richness of their ontology of genres, actors, producers, and more. You likely have discovered a new show or movie thanks in part to Netflix’s ontology. You can imagine how personalization and recommendation systems rely heavily on an optimal ontology to serve up content according to who you are and what you’ve engaged with in their systems.

sections.heading: Ontologies structure concepts and information
sections.text: There’s a spectrum of types of knowledge organization systems that you may already know about. A simple system might be a pick list, which consists of a list of controlled values. A taxonomy is a variation that includes some hierarchy. Both of these data types are already available in Salesforce. As we move along the spectrum, we get systems with more richness. A thesaurus has a similar structure as a taxonomy, but contains a parent-child structure and generic associated relationships to other parts of that structure. Finally, we have an ontology, which consists of a graph or network structure that describes a combination of classes. It has more robust and extensive descriptors for the relationship types. But what pick lists, taxonomies, and thesaurus systems don’t have is the flexibility to grow as your systems grow and evolve. There is more complexity in reorganizing hierarchies as you introduce new instances. That’s where ontology comes in as a graph or network structure — nodes and relationships that can expand easily. The complexity of these concepts and their relationships become a broader representation of a domain that includes richer rules for inference andreasoning. No doubt, this is a heady subject that gets technical really fast. But if you’ve ever played theSix Degrees of Kevin Bacongame and had to explain how a given actor is directly or indirectly connected to Kevin Bacon, you have a high-level sense of how an ontology works.

sections.heading: Anatomy of an ontology
sections.text: What makes an ontology? Let’s explore the anatomy of an ontology with an example that comes from healthcare: A class is a collection of related objects, individuals, or instances. Assign metadata attributes at the class level so that any class or subclass can inherit those attributes. We can define a person, a disease, and a test in a medical setting. The classes we define serve as the organizing metadata framework for the scope of all our ontologies. Individuals are instances of the class or subclass. In this example, a person is the higher order class, a patient is a subclass, and John Doe is an instance, or an individual or patient. From a machine perspective, you can infer that John Doe is a patient and a person. Likewise, Jane Smith is an oncologist and person. If there’s any metadata about Jane Smith, like an honorific, we know that her title is Dr. Jane Smith. Axioms codify in the structure what is the truth about the domain being modeled. It can be expressed in the attributes of the classes or directly in the relationships. In this example, cancer is a subclass of disease. It’s a subclass because we also know there are many types of cancer which could be considered instances. A patient can have at most one primary physician. This type of metadata could be expressed as an axiom directly on the relationship. Another type of axiom would be the expression of dosage for medication that can be expressed as additional nodes in the structure related to medications. Relationships convey meaning. For instance, we haven’t: identified any of the durable medical equipment,modeled the health insurance benefits that would cover for this condition,or itemized all the symptoms or all the body systems involved in this snapshot. So those relationship connections without context provide no meaning. Once you apply the object properties or relationships, you get content, and you get a new level of understanding of how things are related.

sections.heading: Invisible but crucial to innovation
sections.text: Optimizing the hidden power of ontologies is the race within the race to create the best-in-class agentic AI technologies. There are numerous decisions anagenthas to make based on the information it can access. All of that information needs to be labeled and connected accurately and consistently in a machine-readable format. Ontology helps to create a common vocabulary and related terms to describe types of information, which helps with natural language processing. It makes information reusable and enables AI to answer questions. Ontologies also work with other ontologies, collectively powering agentic AI to do its best to deliver what you ask of it. It’s daunting but crucial work to harness metadata. Knowing how to organize troves of information so that your digital workforce can use it to make decisions means better personalization and more precise results.

raw_html:
When you stream music or movies and get suggested playlists or genres based on your habits, you’re benefitting from a type of knowledge organization system called ontology. Maybe you’ve come across this term recently. It’s a hot topic because agentic AI relies on a map of metadata relationships – the data about the data – in order to make decisions. 

So, what is ontology, how does it work, and why does it matter? Let’s dig into the basics of this discipline and its role in agentic experience design.

What we’ll cover:

What is ontology?
Ontologies structure concepts and information
Anatomy of an ontology
Invisible but crucial to innovation

What is ontology?

In the world of AI, ontology is a system for organizing information so computers can understand and use that data to make decisions on the output. To state this more formally, an ontology structures concepts and information through the description of classes, properties, attributes, relationships, and logical axioms. Ontologies form data models for knowledge graphs, ensuring consistency and understanding.

If that feels academic, it’s because it is. Ontology actually evolved from a philosophical discipline — basically, categorizing things in the universe to understand how they relate to one another. Today, it’s a practical tool for structuring knowledge. It’s now related to information and library science, or the study of how to manage, organize, access, preserve, and share information. (Ask a librarian about ontology and watch them light up.)

Without ontologies, your experience using any number of digital platforms would be chaotic. Here are two familiar examples of well-designed ontologies that shape your user experience (UX) and enjoyment: 

  • Spotify has ontologies for music genres, with lots of metadata about albums, artists, and how they’re organized in various playlists. These ontologies help power so many rich playlists and stations where they can dynamically generate these experiences purely based on what music you’ve already engaged in.
  • Netflix optimizes its recommendation engines with the richness of their ontology of genres, actors, producers, and more. You likely have discovered a new show or movie thanks in part to Netflix’s ontology. 

You can imagine how personalization and recommendation systems rely heavily on an optimal ontology to serve up content according to who you are and what you’ve engaged with in their systems.

Take a Deeper Dive

Two Types of Ontologies Your AI Agents Need to Be Trustworthy

How Generative Development Flips the Role of Designers

Welcome to a New Era of Experience with AI Agents

Design Jobs Are Getting an AI Makeover

Ontologies structure concepts and information

There’s a spectrum of types of knowledge organization systems that you may already know about. A simple system might be a pick list, which consists of a list of controlled values. A taxonomy is a variation that includes some hierarchy. Both of these data types are already available in Salesforce.

As we move along the spectrum, we get systems with more richness. A thesaurus has a similar structure as a taxonomy, but contains a parent-child structure and generic associated relationships to other parts of that structure. Finally, we have an ontology, which consists of a graph or network structure that describes a combination of classes. It has more robust and extensive descriptors for the relationship types.

But what pick lists, taxonomies, and thesaurus systems don’t have is the flexibility to grow as your systems grow and evolve. There is more complexity in reorganizing hierarchies as you introduce new instances. That’s where ontology comes in as a graph or network structure — nodes and relationships that can expand easily. The complexity of these concepts and their relationships become a broader representation of a domain that includes richer rules for inference and reasoning. 

No doubt, this is a heady subject that gets technical really fast. But if you’ve ever played the Six Degrees of Kevin Bacon game and had to explain how a given actor is directly or indirectly connected to Kevin Bacon, you have a high-level sense of how an ontology works.

Anatomy of an ontology

What makes an ontology? Let’s explore the anatomy of an ontology with an example that comes from healthcare:

Class

A class is a collection of related objects, individuals, or instances. Assign metadata attributes at the class level so that any class or subclass can inherit those attributes. We can define a person, a disease, and a test in a medical setting. The classes we define serve as the organizing metadata framework for the scope of all our ontologies.

Individual

Individuals are instances of the class or subclass. In this example, a person is the higher order class, a patient is a subclass, and John Doe is an instance, or an individual or patient. From a machine perspective, you can infer that John Doe is a patient and a person. Likewise, Jane Smith is an oncologist and person. If there’s any metadata about Jane Smith, like an honorific, we know that her title is Dr. Jane Smith.

Axiom

Axioms codify in the structure what is the truth about the domain being modeled. It can be expressed in the attributes of the classes or directly in the relationships. In this example, cancer is a subclass of disease. It’s a subclass because we also know there are many types of cancer which could be considered instances.

A patient can have at most one primary physician. This type of metadata could be expressed as an axiom directly on the relationship. Another type of axiom would be the expression of dosage for medication that can be expressed as additional nodes in the structure related to medications. 

Relationships

Relationships convey meaning. For instance, we haven’t:

  • identified any of the durable medical equipment,
  • modeled the health insurance benefits that would cover for this condition,
  • or itemized all the symptoms or all the body systems involved in this snapshot. 

So those relationship connections without context provide no meaning. Once you apply the object properties or relationships, you get content, and you get a new level of understanding of how things are related.

Invisible but crucial to innovation

Optimizing the hidden power of ontologies is the race within the race to create the best-in-class agentic AI technologies. There are numerous decisions an agent has to make based on the information it can access. All of that information needs to be labeled and connected accurately and consistently in a machine-readable format.

Ontology helps to create a common vocabulary and related terms to describe types of information, which helps with natural language processing. It makes information reusable and enables AI to answer questions. Ontologies also work with other ontologies, collectively powering agentic AI to do its best to deliver what you ask of it.

It’s daunting but crucial work to harness metadata. Knowing how to organize troves of information so that your digital workforce can use it to make decisions means better personalization and more precise results.
